name: Shinto Ritual
shortDescription: Shinto rituals are practices performed to communicate with and honor the kami (spirits or deities) in Shinto, the indigenous religion of Japan. These rituals aim to purify, appease, and seek blessings from the kami.
description: Shinto rituals are central to the practice of Shinto and encompass a wide range of ceremonies, festivals (matsuri), and daily practices. These rituals serve as a means of communication and interaction between humans and the kami, the spirits or deities believed to inhabit natural elements, ancestors, and abstract concepts. Purification (harai) is a fundamental aspect of many Shinto rituals, aiming to cleanse individuals and spaces of impurities (kegare) that can obstruct communication with the kami or bring misfortune. Offerings (heihaku) are presented to the kami, often including food, sake, and sacred objects, as a sign of respect and gratitude. Prayers (norito) are recited to convey requests, express gratitude, or narrate myths. Dances (kagura) and music are also integral parts of many Shinto rituals, believed to entertain and invoke the kami. Rituals are performed at shrines (jinja) by priests (kannushi), who are trained in the proper procedures and protocols. The specific rituals and their significance can vary depending on the shrine, the kami being venerated, and the purpose of the ceremony. Important rituals include purification rites, festivals celebrating seasonal events or specific kami, and ceremonies marking life cycle transitions. The story of Amaterasu hiding in a cave highlights the importance of ritual, as the other kami performed dances and music to entice her out, restoring light to the world. Shinto rituals emphasize harmony with nature, respect for ancestors, and the pursuit of purity and well-being.

sources.title: Living Shinto: Creating a Shrine Community in the Modern World
sources.author: Robert S. Ellwood
